threats.title: JavaScript Sandbox Escape via notevil — routes/b2bOrder.ts:23
threats.scenario: An attacker with a valid (or forged) B2B JWT submits a crafted JavaScript expression as `orderLinesData` that exploits the notevil AST-evaluator's known sandbox escape vectors. Because `notevil` is not hardened against adversarial inputs, a sufficiently crafted payload can break out of the `vm.createContext` sandbox and execute arbitrary code as the Node.js process. This achieves full server-side RCE — reading environment secrets, pivoting to internal services, or installing persistence. The notevil library ships with `@ts-expect-error FIXME` in the import comment, indicating the maintainers are aware it lacks proper type safety and RCE-safety guarantees.
threats.evidence_summary: Line 23 of routes/b2bOrder.ts calls `vm.runInContext('safeEval(orderLinesData)', sandbox, { timeout: 2000 })` where `orderLinesData` is the unsanitised request body field, and `safeEval` is the `notevil` library's `eval` function — a known incomplete sandbox.
threats.impact_description: Full server-side code execution as the Node.js process user, enabling credential theft, lateral movement to internal services, and persistent backdoor installation.
threats.controls_in_place: 2000ms vm execution timeout; notevil AST-based expression evaluator (partial sandbox, not RCE-safe)
threats.mitigation_title: Replace notevil/vm sandbox with server-side JSON schema validation of orderLinesData

steps: Remove the `notevil` import and `vm.runInContext` call from routes/b2bOrder.ts entirely — do not use any JavaScript expression evaluator for business-logic input.
Define an explicit JSON schema (using `zod` or `joi`) for the expected order line structure and validate `body.orderLinesData` against it before processing.

If dynamic expressions are genuinely required by the B2B API contract, isolate evaluation in a dedicated sandboxed child process (Node.js `--experimental-vm-modules` with a dedicated worker_thread) with no access to the parent process scope, and enforce strict input size limits.
Add an integration test that submits a known notevil sandbox-escape payload and asserts a 400/422 response, not code execution.

threats.title: Hardcoded RSA Private Key Enables Offline JWT Forgery — lib/insecurity.ts:23
threats.scenario: The RSA private key used to sign all application JWT tokens is committed in plaintext in `lib/insecurity.ts:23`. Any party with repository read access (including all contributors to this open-source project) can extract this key and forge JWT tokens for any user identity or role. An attacker creates a JWT claiming to be an admin or any legitimate B2B customer and presents it to `POST /b2b/v2/orders` — the `isAuthorized()` middleware at server.ts:423 verifies the RS256 signature with the public key and accepts the forged token, rendering the JWT authentication control completely ineffective.
threats.evidence_summary: lib/insecurity.ts line 23 contains the full 1024-bit RSA private key PEM block as a string literal; line 54 shows `isAuthorized()` uses `expressJwt({ secret: publicKey })` — the pairing key is public knowledge.
threats.impact_description: Authentication control for the entire application is neutralised — any attacker can impersonate any user or admin and access all JWT-protected endpoints, not just the B2B API.
threats.controls_in_place: RS256 algorithm used (asymmetric); public key used for verification only
threats.mitigation_title: Rotate hardcoded RSA private key to environment-injected secret and revoke committed key

steps: Generate a new RSA-2048 or RSA-4096 key pair: `openssl genrsa -out jwt_private.pem 2048 && openssl rsa -pubout -in jwt_private.pem -out jwt_public.pem`.

Remove the hardcoded `privateKey` string literal from lib/insecurity.ts and load it from an environment variable: `const privateKey = process.env.JWT_PRIVATE_KEY ?? (() => { throw new Error('JWT_PRIVATE_KEY not set') })()`.
Store the private key in a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or at minimum a `.env` file excluded from git via `.gitignore`) — never in source control.
Treat the committed key as permanently compromised: rotate all issued tokens (invalidate existing sessions) and rotate the key pair immediately on deployment.
Add a startup assertion that rejects any key value matching the committed PEM prefix to prevent accidental reuse.

threats.title: No Rate Limiting on B2B Orders Endpoint Enables Event-Loop Exhaustion — server.ts:645
threats.scenario: The route `POST /b2b/v2/orders` (registered at server.ts:645) is protected only by JWT authentication (`isAuthorized()` at server.ts:423) with no rate limiting middleware. An attacker holding a forged JWT (trivial given the committed private key) can flood the endpoint with concurrent requests. Each request spawns a `vm.runInContext` execution with a 2000ms timeout, pinning a V8 context per request for up to 2 seconds. Sending 50 concurrent requests sustains ~50 parallel vm contexts consuming 100 seconds of combined Node.js event-loop time, effectively blocking the single-threaded event loop and making the entire application unresponsive to legitimate users. The 503 response path (lines 27-30) confirms this DoS vector is an intentional challenge (`rceOccupyChallenge`) but the absence of rate limiting means it is trivially exploitable without any challenge-solve intent.
threats.evidence_summary: server.ts:645 registers `app.post('/b2b/v2/orders', b2bOrder())` with no preceding `rateLimit()` call; grep of server.ts confirms rate limiting is applied to `/rest/user/reset-password` (line 343) and two-factor auth endpoints (lines 458/465/471) but not to any `/b2b/` path.
threats.impact_description: Node.js event-loop saturation causing full application unavailability for all users for the duration of the attack, with no authentication required beyond a single forged JWT.
threats.controls_in_place: 2000ms vm execution timeout per request; JWT authentication required

steps: Import and configure `express-rate-limit` for the B2B endpoint with a conservative window and max: e.g. 10 requests per minute per IP.
Register the rate limiter middleware before the `b2bOrder()` handler at server.ts:645.
Consider adding a per-JWT-subject rate limit (using `keyGenerator: (req) => req.auth?.sub ?? req.ip`) to prevent distributed DoS from a single forged identity across multiple IPs.

threats.title: Missing Structured Audit Log for B2B Order Execution Events — routes/b2bOrder.ts
threats.scenario: The B2B order handler at routes/b2bOrder.ts records no structured security event when an order is accepted or when a sandbox execution error occurs. The only logging in place is HTTP-level access logging via `morgan('combined')` at server.ts:338, which captures the HTTP method and URL but not the authenticated actor's identity from the JWT, the contents of `orderLinesData`, the order number assigned, or whether a sandbox error occurred. A B2B customer can therefore deny placing a particular order, and the operator has no cryptographically-bound, actor-attributed audit trail to prove otherwise. Post-incident forensic reconstruction of RCE attempts is also severely hampered.
threats.evidence_summary: routes/b2bOrder.ts contains no import of a logger and no structured log call; the sole logging path is morgan at server.ts:338 which emits HTTP combined-format lines without actor identity or payload.
threats.impact_description: Inability to attribute specific order-processing events or RCE attempts to a particular B2B actor, preventing fraud investigation and regulatory compliance for financial transactions.
threats.controls_in_place: HTTP access log via morgan (method, URL, status, user-agent only)
threats.mitigation_title: Add structured audit logging of actor identity, orderLinesData hash, and outcome to routes/b2bOrder.ts

steps: In routes/b2bOrder.ts, import the application's logger (e.g. `winston`) and emit a structured log entry on each order request including: JWT subject (`req.auth?.sub`), order number, SHA-256 hash of `orderLinesData` (not raw payload — avoid logging potentially malicious expressions), HTTP status, and timestamp.
Log sandbox errors with the error class/message at WARN level, including the actor subject and a truncated/hashed payload, so RCE attempts are detectable in log analysis.
Route audit logs to an append-only sink (e.g. a dedicated log group, Splunk, or ELK) separate from the application process to prevent log tampering by an attacker who achieves RCE.

threats.title: Sandbox Error Details Leaked to B2B API Callers — routes/b2bOrder.ts:32
threats.scenario: When `safeEval(orderLinesData)` throws an error that is not a timeout (lines 25-33 of b2bOrder.ts), the raw error object is passed to `next(err)`. Express's default error handler (or any generic error middleware) will serialize this error — potentially including the error class name, message text from within the notevil evaluator, and stack frames — and return it in the HTTP response body. An attacker probing for sandbox escape vectors can use this error oracle to fingerprint the evaluator version, understand which expression constructs are rejected, and refine their escape payload iteratively.
threats.evidence_summary: routes/b2bOrder.ts line 32 calls `next(err)` with the raw caught error from `vm.runInContext`, with no sanitization before forwarding to Express error middleware.
threats.impact_description: Disclosure of internal evaluator error messages and stack frames to API callers, enabling fingerprinting of the notevil version and iterative sandbox-escape payload refinement.
threats.controls_in_place: `utils.getErrorMessage(err)` used for string matching (line 26) but the raw `err` object is forwarded to next() on line 32
threats.mitigation_title: Sanitize sandbox errors before forwarding to Express error middleware in b2bOrder.ts

steps: In the catch block at routes/b2bOrder.ts:32, replace `next(err)` with `next(new Error('Invalid order expression'))` — a generic error message that reveals nothing about the internal evaluator.
Log the original error server-side (with the actor identity and payload hash) before discarding it from the response path.

Ensure the Express error-handling middleware at the application level also strips stack traces from 500 responses sent to clients in production (set `NODE_ENV=production` or use `helmet()` error filtering).

threats.title: Hardcoded Ethereum Wallet Mnemonic Exposes Wallet Private Key — routes/checkKeys.ts:10
threats.scenario: The Ethereum wallet mnemonic `'purpose betray marriage blame crunch monitor spin slide donate sport lift clutch'` is hardcoded at routes/checkKeys.ts:10. Anyone with access to the repository can derive the HD wallet's private key using `HDNodeWallet.fromPhrase(mnemonic).privateKey` and take full control of any assets held at the derived address. The `POST /rest/web3/submitKey` endpoint (server.ts:638) accepts a private key guess and compares it directly against this derived key — the comparison logic at checkKeys.ts:18 (`req.body.privateKey === privateKey`) also leaks timing information about the key value to brute-forcers, though the committed mnemonic makes that moot.
threats.evidence_summary: routes/checkKeys.ts line 10 hard-codes the BIP-39 mnemonic string; the derived private key is computed from it at runtime and compared in plaintext at line 18.
threats.impact_description: Any repository reader can derive and exfiltrate the Ethereum wallet private key, enabling theft of all ETH and ERC-20/ERC-721 tokens held at the wallet address.
threats.controls_in_place: None — mnemonic is plaintext in source
threats.mitigation_title: Move Ethereum wallet mnemonic to an environment variable and remove it from source

steps: Remove the hardcoded mnemonic string from routes/checkKeys.ts and load it from `process.env.ETH_WALLET_MNEMONIC`.
Rotate the Ethereum wallet immediately — generate a new mnemonic and transfer any assets to the new address; treat the committed mnemonic as permanently compromised.
Add `ETH_WALLET_MNEMONIC` to `.env.example` with a placeholder and to `.gitignore` / secrets scanning rules to prevent future commits.

Replace the timing-unsafe direct string comparison (`=== privateKey`) with a constant-time comparison to prevent key oracle attacks: `crypto.timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(req.body.privateKey), Buffer.from(privateKey))`.
remediation.code_example:
```typescript
// routes/checkKeys.ts — AFTER fix
const mnemonic = process.env.ETH_WALLET_MNEMONIC
  ?? (() => { throw new Error('ETH_WALLET_MNEMONIC not set') })()
const mnemonicWallet = HDNodeWallet.fromPhrase(mnemonic)
const privateKey = mnemonicWallet.privateKey
// ... constant-time comparison:
import crypto from 'node:crypto'
const isMatch = req.body.privateKey?.length === privateKey.length &&
  crypto.timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(req.body.privateKey), Buffer.from(privateKey))
```
